notes: General category: computing architectures designed to prevent any interruption from hardware failures.
Four microprocessors arranged in two pairs with hardware comparator; failure handled without software awareness or lost clock cycles.
Software-based fault tolerance using processor pairs and application-level checkpointing; required specialized programmer effort.
First Stratus product; announced December 1981; rebranded by IBM as the IBM System/88 under OEM agreement.
IBM-branded version of Stratus FT200; OEM deal enabled Stratus marketing message 'The Technology IBM Chose.'
OEM deal in which IBM rebranded Stratus FT200 as IBM System/88; strategically neutralized startup-risk objections.
Built on Motorola 68020 processors; Kastner led market and product team for this announcement; preserved lockstep architecture.
Microprocessor used in Stratus XA 2000; enabled dramatic performance gains vs. previous Stratus hardware.
Standard programming language on Stratus/System/88; Kastner's 'zero-code advantage' message: standard COBOL worked without fault-tolerance modifications.
Also cited alongside COBOL as standard language usable without modification on Stratus fault-tolerant hardware.
Central market use case for fault-tolerant hardware; Kastner's 1987 Yankee Group report predicted OLTP price-performance explosion.
Telecom signaling protocol; Stratus became embedded infrastructure for SS7 signaling systems after telecom market breakthrough.
Approach contrasted with software fault tolerance (Tandem); hides failures from application software entirely.
Referenced as example of non-fault-tolerant mainframe that crashed on component failure; central to Kastner's market problem statement.
Kastner's downtime-cost calculator framework: quantify cost of downtime per hour vs. hardware price premium to justify fault-tolerant investment.
100-page report published January 1987; predicted microprocessor-based OLTP price-performance explosion and mainframe displacement via Moore's Law.
Kastner invoked Moore's Law as driver for microprocessor OLTP economics overtaking mainframes in the 1987 Yankee Group report.
